ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'biryani' class (FOODON_00002694). No specific vegetable dum biryani subclass exists; closest_parent match applied.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKW covers biryani as a rice dish category. No vegetable-specific dum biryani code exists; closest_parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for vegetable dum biryani. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular prepared-dish codes at this level.

preparation_method: layering of parboiled rice and vegetable masala
main_ingredients: mixed vegetables (carrot, peas, potato, cauliflower, beans)
whole spices (cardamom, cloves, bay leaf, cinnamon, star anise)

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Sabzi Dum Biryani is approximately 400 g, comprising a generous mound of saffron-scented basmati rice layered with spiced mixed vegetables, typically served with raita or curry sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Vegetable Biryani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 revised edition), composite rice-vegetable dish entry; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 'Rice, white, long-grain, cooked' (FDC ID 168878) and 'Vegetables, mixed, cooked' for component validation
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides a vegetable biryani composite entry. Values have been adapted to reflect the typical restaurant-style dum biryani preparation with ghee, saffron, and fried onion garnish. Sodium estimate accounts for added salt in restaurant preparation (~400 mg/100 g). USDA SR Legacy used for cross-validation of basmati rice and vegetable component macros.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; basmati rice cooked (GI 57) used as primary analogous anchor for vegetable biryani
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for vegetable dum biryani in the University of Sydney GI Database. Basmati rice (cooked, white, long-grain) has a well-established GI of 57 (medium) per Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables and the University of Sydney GI Database. Vegetable biryani is predominantly basmati rice (~60% by weight); the addition of vegetables, ghee, and spices is expected to modestly lower the effective GI relative to plain rice due to fat and fibre content. GI of 57 is therefore a conservative upper-bound analogous estimate. GL per serving = (57 × 23.8 × 400) / (100 × 100) = 34.2 g per 400 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~390 mg/100 g; ~1560 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single 400 g restaurant serving of Sabzi Dum Biryani contributes approximately 1560 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (GI 57) and high glycemic load per restaurant serving (~34 g GL)
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI of 57 and high glycemic load of approximately 34 g per 400 g restaurant serving make Sabzi Dum Biryani a food that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume in controlled portions (ideally ≤200 g), paired with a high-fibre side such as raita or salad to attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from ghee (~1.8 g/100 g) and vegetable-dominant composition
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Sabzi Dum Biryani is a vegetable-based dish with moderate saturated fat from ghee; individuals with cardiovascular disease may request preparation with vegetable oil instead of ghee to reduce saturated fat intake, and should be mindful of the sodium load in restaurant portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sabzi Dum Biryani is a classic North Indian vegetarian rice dish prepared using the traditional dum (slow-steam) cooking technique, in which parboiled long-grain basmati rice is layered with a richly spiced vegetable masala and sealed in a heavy pot to cook in its own steam. The dish originates from the Mughlai culinary tradition of the Indian subcontinent and is widely served across Indian restaurants in Singapore. The vegetable filling typically includes carrots, green peas, potatoes, cauliflower, and French beans, cooked with onion, tomato, yoghurt, and an aromatic blend of whole spices (cardamom, cloves, bay leaf, cinnamon, star anise) and ground spices (cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli). Saffron dissolved in warm milk or water is drizzled over the top layer of rice to impart a golden hue and floral fragrance. The dish is finished with fried onion (birista), fresh mint, and coriander leaves. The texture is fluffy and aromatic, with each grain of rice remaining distinct and lightly coated in spiced ghee. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 148 kcal, 3.5 g protein, 4.2 g fat (of which 1.8 g is saturated, primarily from ghee), and 23.8 g carbohydrate with 1.6 g dietary fibre. The glycemic index is estimated at 57 (medium) based on the basmati rice component, with a glycemic load of approximately 34 g per standard 400 g restaurant serving. Sodium content is elevated in restaurant preparations (~390 mg/100 g), making portion control important for individuals managing hypertension or Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian, halal-certified in most Singapore Indian restaurants, and gluten-free, though it contains dairy (ghee and yoghurt).
common_user_queries: How many calories in vegetable biryani?
Is dum biryani good for diabetics?
What vegetables are in sabzi biryani?
What is the GI of biryani?
Can I eat biryani if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Sabzi Dum Biryani exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN vegetable biryani composite entry (2017) with adjustments for restaurant-style preparation including ghee and added salt. GI is an analogous estimate based on basmati rice (GI 57) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international GI tables. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. The food_name has been normalised to 'Sabzi Dum Biryani' as the canonical English name; 'Gulzar' is a restaurant brand qualifier and not part of the dish identity. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) vegetable biryani entry; sodium is estimated at the higher end (~390 mg/100 g) to reflect typical restaurant salt usage. 3. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate based on cooked basmati rice; actual GI of the composite dish may be slightly lower due to fat and fibre from vegetables and ghee. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero, as this is a vegetarian dish containing no meat, fish, or eggs; trace amounts from dairy (yoghurt, ghee) are below detection threshold and reported as 0.0. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA source data for this dish type. 6. Serving size of 400 g is estimated from typical North Indian restaurant biryani portions in Singapore; actual restaurant portions may vary between 350–450 g. 7. Ontology codes use closest_parent matches as no vegetable dum biryani-specific codes exist in FoodOn or FoodEx2. 8. Allergen note: contains dairy (ghee, yoghurt); may contain traces of nuts in some restaurant preparations. 9. vitamin_d_mcg is -1.0 and selenium_mcg is -1.0 — these nutrients were not reported by the primary source (ICMR-NIN) for this dish and are not typically significant components.
